serving.natural_language_portion: One medium fresh apricot weighs approximately 35 g and provides about 14 kcal, making it a light and nutritious snack portion.

nutrient_source.title: Apricots, raw — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrient and primary micronutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for raw fresh apricot, consistent with input record provenance (confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrient fields not reported by HPB (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, vitamins, minerals) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09021 (Apricots, raw).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 34 (low) for fresh apricot is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. GL per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 34 × (7.7 g/100 g × 35 g) / 100 = 34 × 2.695 / 100 ≈ 0.92, rounded to 0.9 for a 35 g medium apricot.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium-free with high potassium (290 mg/100 g) supporting DASH dietary pattern
hypertension.guidance: Fresh apricot contains 0 mg sodium and 290 mg potassium per 100 g, making it an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension following the DASH dietary pattern; consume fresh rather than dried to avoid concentrated sugar and sulphite additives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (34) with intrinsic sugar moderated by dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fresh apricot has a low glycaemic index of 34 and a glycaemic load of approximately 0.9 per medium fruit, making it a suitable fruit choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when limited to 3–4 fruits per serving; dried apricots should be avoided due to concentrated sugar and elevated glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol-free, low saturated fat, high potassium, and rich in beta-carotene and vitamin E antioxidants
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fresh apricot is cholesterol-free with negligible saturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), provides 290 mg potassium and antioxidants including beta-carotene (96 mcg RAE vitamin A) and vitamin E (0.89 mg), supporting cardiovascular health as part of a fruit-rich dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in beta-carotene and vitamin C antioxidants; lacks omega-3 fatty acids and does not meet the >3 g/100 g whole-food fibre threshold for primary anti-inflammatory classification
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fresh apricot provides beta-carotene and vitamin C which may help neutralise free radicals associated with joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis, but it does not supply omega-3 fatty acids or sufficient fibre (>3 g/100 g) to qualify as a primary anti-inflammatory food; choose fresh over dried to avoid sulphite preservatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Apricot (Prunus armeniaca) is a small, round stone fruit belonging to the Rosaceae family, believed to have originated in the Tian Shan mountain range of Central Asia — in the region spanning modern-day Armenia and Iran — before spreading along ancient Silk Road trade routes to the Mediterranean, Middle East, South Asia, and temperate zones worldwide. The fruit is characterised by its distinctive golden-orange to deep amber skin, which is smooth to slightly velvety and fuzzy to the touch, encasing tender, juicy flesh that ranges from pale yellow to vivid orange depending on variety and ripeness. The flavour profile is a harmonious balance of natural sweetness and mild, refreshing tartness, with aromatic floral notes that intensify as the fruit ripens. The texture is soft yet firm enough to hold its shape when freshly picked, becoming more yielding and fragrant as it reaches peak ripeness. Fresh apricots are typically consumed raw as a snack or incorporated into fruit salads, breakfast bowls, and desserts; they are also widely used in jams, preserves, chutneys, and compotes, as well as in savoury applications such as tagines and glazes for poultry and pork in Mediterranean and Middle Eastern cuisines. Dried apricots are a popular preserved form but carry a significantly higher sugar concentration and are often treated with sulphite preservatives. Nutritionally, fresh apricot is a low-calorie fruit providing approximately 40 kcal per 100 g. It contains minimal protein (0.2 g/100 g) and fat (0.4 g/100 g), with a moderate carbohydrate content of 7.7 g/100 g, of which 7.6 g is naturally occurring intrinsic sugar. The dietary fibre content of 1.5 g per 100 g plays a key role in moderating the glycaemic response, contributing to its low glycaemic index of 34 as confirmed by the University of Sydney GI Database. Apricot is notably sodium-free and an excellent source of potassium (290 mg/100 g), making it highly beneficial for individuals managing blood pressure within the DASH dietary framework. It is a significant source of provitamin A in the form of beta-carotene (96 mcg RAE/100 g), vitamin C (10 mg/100 g), and vitamin E (0.89 mg/100 g), all contributing potent antioxidant activity that supports immune function and may help reduce oxidative stress. Clinically, fresh apricot is well-suited for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (GI 34, GL ~0.9 per medium fruit), hypertension (sodium-free, potassium-rich), and cardiovascular disease (cholesterol-free, low saturated fat, antioxidant-rich) when consumed in appropriate controlled portions. It is naturally plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and allergen-free, making it broadly suitable across diverse dietary requirements.
common_user_queries: Can I eat apricot if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of apricot?
How many calories in a fresh apricot?
Is apricot good for heart health?
How many apricots can a diabetic eat?
Is fresh apricot better than dried apricot?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and primary micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved source, confidence 0.95 per input record). Secondary nutrient fields cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09021 (Apricots, raw). GI value of 34 confirmed in University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). All values are internally consistent and plausible for fresh raw apricot.
notes: Sodium is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g by HPB — confirmed zero per approved source; naturally negligible in fresh whole fruit. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g — naturally zero as this is a whole plant food containing no animal-derived components. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg/100 g — naturally zero as plant foods do not synthesise cobalamin. Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg/100 g — naturally zero for this fruit as confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09021. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g — naturally zero in unprocessed fresh fruit. Saturated fat (0.03 g), monounsaturated fat (0.17 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.08 g), and all vitamin and mineral values not reported by HPB are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09021 (Apricots, raw) as a cross-reference. The rheumatoid arthritis suitability assessment from the input record has been corrected: the input incorrectly set suitable=false while the reason text described positive antioxidant properties; the record has been reclassified as moderate concern with nuanced guidance reflecting both the antioxidant benefits and the absence of omega-3 fatty acids. GL per serving = GI (34) × carbohydrate per serving (7.7 g/100 g × 35 g serving = 2.695 g) / 100 = 0.92, rounded to 0.9.
